{{COURSE_CODE}} / {{COURSE_NAME}}
Materials Hands-on FAQ Setup
Test-Driven · AI-Assisted · Weekly

{{COURSE_NAME}}, weekly.

Prepare with AI. Show your understanding without it. Each week below is a learning sheet you study openly, paired with a closed-book test that asks whether you can use the ideas unaided.

Course
{{COURSE_CODE}}
Textbook
{{TEXTBOOK_SHORT}}
Method
TDAA
§ 01

Weekly materials

Each entry opens a learning sheet — a guided walk through the week's textbook scope, with prompts you can paste into any LLM and a validation set that mirrors the test's structure without revealing it.

§ 02

Hands-on, in four steps

The shortest path from "I opened this page" to "I'm ready for Friday's test." Roughly forty minutes per week, plus practice.

  1. Read the learning sheet

    Pick this week's row above and read straight through. The sheet is built as a single arc — definitions, intuition, worked example, proof sketch — so resist the temptation to skim.

    Symbols and terms are introduced before they are used; if you hit one that feels unfamiliar, it's defined a paragraph or two earlier, not later.

  2. Talk to an AI tutor

    Each sheet ships with copy-paste prompts marked #prompt. Open DeepSeek, Claude, or any chat model, paste the textbook chapter, then paste the prompt. 

    I'm studying {{COURSE_NAME}}, week N.
Here is the textbook chapter and the learning sheet.
Walk me through example 2 step by step,
then quiz me on the definition of X.

    Treat AI as a patient TA, not a homework solver. The goal is to come out able to explain the idea to a classmate.

  3. Run the validation set

    Every learning sheet has a paired validation document of the same shape and difficulty as the closed-book test. Solve it on paper, with no AI and no notes, in roughly the time the real test will give you.

    If you stumble on a question, that's signal — go back to the relevant section of the sheet, do another AI-assisted pass, and try again the next day.

  4. Show up to the weekly test

    Closed book, closed notes, closed AI. Tests are scored out of 130 points but capped at 100, so two missed questions still leave you a perfect grade. The cap is intentional — it keeps the gate strict without making it cruel.

§ 03

Frequently asked

Questions students and instructors raise in the first two weeks of any TDAA course. If yours is missing, ask in class — we'll add it.

What is TDAA, exactly?

Test-Driven, AI-Assisted learning. AI is allowed while you study, practice, and prepare. AI is not allowed during the regular closed-book tests that grade the course. The pair is the whole point: AI lowers the cost of patient explanation, and the gate keeps the grade tied to your own understanding.

Am I allowed to use AI for homework?

Yes. The learning sheets explicitly include AI prompts. Cite the tools and prompts you used, the same way you'd cite a textbook. The honesty norm is "name your sources," not "work alone."

Why a weekly test instead of a midterm and a final?

Because if the gate appears only once, you can postpone real learning for weeks. If it appears every week, AI-assisted study becomes preparation for an unaided performance, and the cost of falling behind never gets large enough to feel hopeless.

Why is the test scored out of 130 but capped at 100?

So that strictness doesn't tip into cruelty. You can miss two full questions and still earn a perfect score. The cap also shifts the conversation in office hours from "how do I squeeze out the last point" to "what did I not understand."

How does the validation set differ from the test?

Same structure, same difficulty, different specific questions. Validation is open — you can use AI, talk to friends, look at the sheet. Test is closed. The point of the validation set is to remove uncertainty about the scope without giving away the questions.

Which AI model should I use?

DeepSeek is free, accepts PDF uploads, and is strong on step-by-step reasoning — a good default. If you have access, Claude is excellent at explanation and proof checking, and Gemini handles the longest chapters. See the setup guide for specifics.

I'm an instructor — how do I fork this?

The repository ships with a /bootstrap Claude Code skill that ingests your textbook, fills in course metadata, and generates a weekly schedule. Then /generate-week N produces a learning sheet, a test, a test variant B, and a validation set per week. See the README on GitHub for the step-by-step instructor guide.

Where does the methodology come from?

From a 13-week run of DSAA 3071, Theory of Computation at HKUST(GZ) in Spring 2026. The paper that documents the run and the harness is the source of every default in this template.
